source_label: Daniel Krawisz — Bitcoin and the Rhetoric of Anarchy
excerpt: The community's preference for soft forks over hard forks is a deep institutional commitment: it preserves the right of any individual operator to opt out of an upgrade without being expelled from the network.
body:
Source excerpt:
The community's preference for soft forks over hard forks is a deep institutional commitment: it preserves the right of any individual operator to opt out of an upgrade without being expelled from the network.

---

Cited by leaf "Consensus rules, soft forks, hard forks":
*Consensus rules* are the conditions under which every full node agrees a block (and the transactions inside it) is valid. They include the proof-of-work target, the block-size and block-weight limits, the script-validation rules, the subsidy schedule, the maximum sigop count, the BIP-30 / BIP-34 historical exceptions, and many more. A node that disagrees on any of these forks itself off the network — it builds and follows a different chain than its peers do [1].

Upgrading the consensus rules is therefore a delicate operation, and Bitcoin distinguishes two flavours.

A *soft fork* tightens the rules: blocks that were valid under the old rules may now be invalid under the new ones, but every block valid under the new rules is also valid under the old. This means old (un-upgraded) nodes still accept the new chain — they just don't enforce the new restrictions. Activation can be done via miner signaling (BIP-9 version bits, BIP-8 forced activation), via flag-day deployment, or via combinations. Examples: P2SH (BIP-16), Segwit (BIP-141), Taproot (BIP-340/341/342) [2].

A *hard fork* loosens or changes the rules in a way that produces blocks invalid under the old rules. Old nodes will reject the new chain as invalid; the network splits unless every node upgrades simultaneously. Hard forks are essentially never done on mainnet because coordinating a simultaneous upgrade across thousands of independent operators is functionally impossible without producing chain splits. The Bitcoin Cash split in 2017 is the canonical example of what happens when a hard-fork attempt fails to achieve consensus: a permanently divergent chain [3].

The asymmetry is deliberate. Soft forks let the protocol evolve without coercing every operator to upgrade in lockstep, which preserves the property that any individual node operator can refuse a change they dislike (by simply not upgrading) without losing access to the chain. Hard forks would require coercion. The Bitcoin community's strong preference for soft forks reflects an institutional commitment to that asymmetry — it is one of the more important norms enforced socially, not by code [2].

In practice this means most upgrades arrive as carefully constructed soft forks that re-purpose previously-unused script flags (`OP_NOP`s, segwit-versioned outputs, taproot leaf versions) to add new functionality without invalidating any historical block.
